item.title: Ha ha joke in bound bois.
item.content:encoded: You could say it might cause a… Resonance Cascade….

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Replacement photo is Abandoned Soviet Over-the-Horizon Radar Array - Chernobyl Exclusion Zone - Northern Ukraine - 08 by Adam Jones from Kelowna, BC, Canada, licensed cc 2.0 (see details here: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Abandoned_Soviet_Over-the-Horizon_Radar_Array_-_Chernobyl_Exclusion_Zone_-_Northern_Ukraine_-_08_(26494245624).jpg)

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the source requires the image to be bought in order to be used, seen here. As such, the image has been removed.

EDIT: The image has been replaced with a cc-compliant alternative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-england-bristol-35353241

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Curious about what's special about Bristol. Guess the writer is a Bristolian.

item.title: Thought that this video related well to the SCP
item.content:encoded:
So when I read this article, I immediately thought that it coincided well with this video about a real like theoretical "end of the universe" scenario, called "Vacuum Decay", especially with the whole "unstoppable chain reaction" bit. My own headcannon would think that this specific set of tones might be what causes this "Vacuum Decay" to start, and I thought I'd share my observation with everyone :P
https://youtu.be/ijFm6DxNVyI

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Finally managed to find this one again. Although there’s a large amount of handwaving involved (like how they found it in the first place), I feel like this is one of my favorite examples of practical containment procedures on the site. Extinguishing an entire whale species is an angle that I never expected.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I heard there was a secret chord that David played to please the Lord…
It's like the opposite of that one Star Trek movie with the ship that speaks whale, and I like that! +1

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Don't forget 2399! 2399 sounds like the perfect example of an existential threat to take out with 1012.

Edit: Responded to the wrong post. I meant to respond to Conwell's post above.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The proposed edit works for me. I say proceed.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timers Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at anytime.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

In reading this article, I immediately thought to myself "what if the Foundation weaponzied it and used it against hostile extraterrestrial threats like SCP-1548 or SCP-2838?" From the comments above, I can see I'm not the only to arrive at this conclusion. It certainly seems like a viable use of the anomalous effect, but on the other hand, if someone fucks it up the consequences would be dire. As such, I am proposing the following edits:

Changing this:

The simultaneous generation of all five tones of SCP-1012 for a duration exceeding a few seconds affects1 the resonance of certain subatomic particles within range, causing them to disintegrate into their constituent elementary particles. Computer modeling predicts that a generation of SCP-1012 within close proximity of a mass, such as an astronomical body, is likely to result in an uncontrollable chain reaction resulting in the disintegration of virtually all matter comprising the mass. According to the model, once such a chain reaction is initiated, it will continue to progress whether or not the tones of SCP-1012 are being generated, until all available mass is affected (i.e., a CK-class scenario).

To this:

The simultaneous generation of all five tones of SCP-1012 for a duration exceeding a few seconds affects2 the resonance of certain subatomic particles within range, causing them to disintegrate into their constituent elementary particles. Computer modeling predicts that a generation of SCP-1012 within close proximity of a mass, such as an astronomical body, is likely to result in an uncontrollable chain reaction resulting in the disintegration of virtually all matter comprising the mass. According to the model, once such a chain reaction is initiated, it will continue to progress whether or not the tones of SCP-1012 are being generated, until all available mass is affected (i.e., a CK-class scenario). Proposals to use SCP-1012 or a modified version as a defense mechanism against hostile extraterrestrial threats, including SCP-1548 and SCP-2838, have been rejected due to the potentially disastrous side effects of SCP-1012 testing.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

Footnotes
1. The described result is predicted according to the cosmological model set forth in Foundation Document 8001.2552.KT.1012. Laboratory testing of the model is prohibited.
2. The described result is predicted according to the cosmological model set forth in Foundation Document 8001.2552.KT.1012. Laboratory testing of the model is prohibited.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The containment procedures make absolutely no sense. An anti-phase of a sound wave doesn't exist unless you're actively cancelling another sound wave. It's nonsensical. The foundation is literally just broadcasting two of these frequencies from basically everything and it makes NO SENSE because that is not the kind of mistake anybody could possibly make.

I have to downvote this because of that, even if I like the concept of the object itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Exactly!

A wave's anti-phase is just the exact same bloody wave, just a fraction of a second earlier or later.

This really should be explained. This is bad.

item.title: Re: Possible improvements?
item.content:encoded: Article's going to stay as-is, but you could really do something (like write a good tale) out of that scenario you're spinning.

item.title: Possible improvements?
item.content:encoded:
I hope this doesn't count as a necropost, as I had a few ideas for this article arise that I think might work.

  • Regarding the B' and D' disruption signals, there's a different way to go about it—phase-modulated signals. As it stands, the article uses only anti-phase signals to counter the problem frequencies. The problem is that what signal is being used as a reference to develop the out-of-phase nature described? Is the signal already present from some external source that the Foundation can reliably suppress it? Or are the jamming signals unwittingly setting up the circumstances for an accidental CK-event? Randomly modulating the phase of the B' and D' radio signals stands a better chance of acting as a jamming signal.
  • What signal levels are required for this CK-event to occur? Do they require the insane power output of a couple hundred fusion reactors dumping power into dishes 10 times Arecibo's size? Or could some hapless hobbyist with a 1kW radio transceiver bring about the end of matter? Having some kind of reference (even if redacted/censored) would help make the threat more believable.
  • The source of this SCP. A theoretical physics model is one thing, but for Keter, there has to have been something to make its containment (and disastrous implications for failure) so strong. I have a possible scenario (not the only one that would work, just an example)…

One day, a radio telescope picks up an unusual radio signal from a star, followed by its disappearance from the sky, and the absence of ANY signal at all from that area, not even background radiation. A Foundation team is deployed to recover the data and push amnestics to the telescope operators, as usual. Review of the data reveals a brief radio transmission at the hydrogen line (1420 MHz), in line with current thinking about extraterrestrial radio communications. Not all of the transmission is decipherable, but there are a few elements that stand out: the presence of the same message header opening and closing the "message", the encoding of the SCP's periods as multiples of the hydrogen transition time (just like on the Voyager probes) and signal intensities in some other "universal" unit, and the repeating of the message multiple times before the total radio silence of the source.

Initially, the Foundation just files the data away for a while. Then, a new event. Same sudden radio transmission from another star, then absolute radio silence, even from noise. Analysis shows the signal differs significantly from the original, but carries the same effective message: the SCP-1210 frequencies and intensities (encoded the same way), and a signal pattern that seems highly reminiscent of a warning or distress signal. This prompts the investigation, the physical model, and the Keter-class procedures.

In addition, this would make for a nice tragic underline to the whale extinction event—they were exterminated because a misinterpretation of the signal suggested that even their relatively low vocalization power was enough to contribute to 1210's effects, and by the time the minimum signal levels were revised, the species was already extinct.

Too drastic for such an older article?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The fact that everyday background noise is potentially connected to the containment of these frequencies totally blows my mind. Also love the distinct SCP-logo in the picture :) +1 naturally

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I want to upvote, but the active noise cancellation bit makes no sense. In the absence of what it is designed to cancel, wouldn't it be indistiguishable from the problematic frequency? It would make far more sense if the counter frequency was different and had a "damping" effect on the chain reaction, like control rods in a nuclear reactor.

Neutral vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think if you mentioned it but put a lot of redacted information and [DATA EXPUNGED] that would be enough for me. Or even an SCP. Maybe just expunge all of it. Please just give me something. That is all I need to +1 this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could this be used to destroy harmful objects in space like 1548?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 but frequency C is scientifically inaccurate. Any frequency represented as 4-digits Hz would be within the normal human hearing range. (20Hz-20KHz) It should be under 20Hz to truly be infrasonic. Derp. Obviously there's a decimal in there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
spikebrennan, I understand, but it is a bit of meme-pollution. I cannot help but to think that the Foundation could very well weaponize 1012, on demand, to destroy any planet they chose to, and that it makes most plans one could conceivably have to have the Foundation invaded sort of moot.

And, of course, I keep comparing the thing to Ender's Game, so… :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't know what Little Doctor was until I looked it up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It has already been said that this is essentially the Little Doctor. I'm not sure how I feel about that.

No vote for now, I may revisit the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Take a space probe with sealed container of air at STP. Inside are air, speakers, battery, simple computer, radio receiver. Also heater to maintain the T. Officially, the probe is going somewhere. Actually, the probe is going somewhere else, distinct for its lack of anything else. Officially, something malfunctions and contact with the probe is lost; a great tragedy for astronomy. Some time later, after the probe is far enough nowhere, away from everything else in interplanetary space, the sealed container of stuff is jettisoned, the speakers remotely activated, the chord played, the result observed by the probe the public was told was lost. Particle physicists and astronomers on earth scratch their heads, trying to puzzle out possible sources of a mysterious neutrino burst from yonder way.

Six people sitting around a computer in a nondescript and otherwise vacant warehouse in Modesto are like, 'Shiiiiit.' The seventh says, "I told you so," and will bring this up the next four times someone argues against rendering a species extinct based entirely on theory. He will be unpopular and mostly wrong.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: God, I love it when we have to extinct a species to contain something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: considering you can set something up to play all 5 notes at the same time… also, it's self propagating, think nuclear fission.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know, I get the concept, but I'm am just really, really unconvinced that this scenario is likely enough to merit all these measures. The main problem I had was that it says "if all 5 frequencies are broadcast at the same time the world will end." That's the entire basis of the danger. But sounds don't travel forever. All the sounds would have to be transmitted not only at the same time, but in roughly the same place. I mean the odds of this happening are simply too high to give merit to. The Earth has been around for 4 billion years and this has never happened? I wonder why? Anyway, the radio towers transmitting B' and D' are enough to prevent the entire XK scenario from happening. You need all 5 so taking out 1 is enough. Honestly, if I were in charge of the Foundation I wouldn't even bother trying with this, it's just so… insignificant.

Another thing, why is this classified as anomalous? I mean I get that the phenomenon is potentially world-ending but this seems fairly scientific, and easy to explain. Fictional of course, but it is within (pseudo-scientific standards at least) acceptable boundaries. Downvoted for too many logical problems

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How exactly can frequency C be subsonic? Considering subsonic means slower than the speed of sound I find it unfathomable how the sound can be slower than itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The main problem I have with this article is the way frequencies A and E are documented. Frequency A is needlessly imprecise. It could be recorded as, "6 cents higher than G#," or whatever you want it to be, but "slightly higher than G#" sounds unsuitable for a scientific document. As for frequency E, there's a similar problem of imprecision. Anything "slightly higher than G#" would also rarely (never) be used in the chromatic musical scale; thus, since it is within range of human hearing, E could have a similar method of description as exemplified for frequency A. I think it's a nice idea for an SCP, but I have to withhold my upvote until this article is edited a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Motivated by a reddit comment I read this back over, and sadly the physics of this really doesn't pass any scrutiny. Active noise cancellation (in fact, any destructive interference of waves) does not and cannot work that way - there's no way to ensure the sound waves remain in antiphase at all points in 3D space unless the antiphase tone is emitted at the same point in space as the original tone, which it isn't. And it fundamentally can't be.

Consider the case for light, which is another kind of wave, after all. You can do destructive interference with light (ask any undergrad physicist), but it would obviously be insanity to try to create "dark zones" by using bulbs that emitted the antiphase of sunlight, or any frequency of light for that matter. But that's what this article is claiming happens with sound. It can't work.

Removing my upvote in the hopes of some kind of patch, since I like nearly everything else about the article but can't in good conscience let a physics error that bad slide.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've heard there was a secret chord, That David played, and it pleased the Lord, But you don't really care for music, do you?
It goes like this, The fourth, the fifth
The minor fall, the major lift
The baffled kings composing Hallelujah

Hallelujah, Hallelujah
Hallelujah, Hallelujah

Your faith was strong but you needed proof
You saw her standing on the roof
Her might in the moonlight overthrew you
You were sat in a kitchen chair,
She broke your throne, and she cut your hair
And from your lips she drew one last Hallelujah

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Through Foundation efforts, Balaenoptera svalbardi became extinct in 1982

This line sells the article for me. Casually making a species extinct strikes me as something the Foundation would do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That still doesn't explain how they found out what this combination of frequencies would do - it doesn't address the problem at all, in my opinion. However, if you tacked on that they were, say, on the ground controlling and fiddling with a bunch of multi-frequency noise makers contained in, I don't know, a space ship far from any celestial body, in order to safely test sonic-based weapons, and then they stumbled upon this batch of frequencies and the ship suddenly went offline (disintegrated), and they sent another ship to see what happened to the first ship and found…I don't know what there would be, powder?…and put two and two together, then I would say the problem is solved. But seriously, that sounds utterly ridiculous, doesn't it? :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That is exactly what I was thinking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me of the Doctor Device from Ender's Game.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And ain't it just like the Foundation to make some desperate attempt to prevent the end of the world, and in the process, make it far more likely instead?

When they figure out their mistake, well, they can shut down their transmitters, but there's no way even the Foundation is going to get all those personal electronics back.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My point is, if there is no B or D being emitted, then B' and D' aren't cancelling anything out. Since B' and D' are at the problem frequencies, emitting them in the absence of B and D actually makes the event more likely to occur, not less.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Noise cancellation (plus a bit of physics handwaving).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aren't the B' and D' waves just as dangerous as B and D would be? Or, to put it another way, aren't B' and D' indistinguishable from B and D?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, what? What does this do? Is it practically the same thing as SCP-084, but in audio form?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was beginning to despair that nobody was going to pick up on that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted for the reference to the Bristol Hum. I thought it was only a matter of time before an SCP used that for something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one. The idea of a dissonance in the universe which can undo that which has been made is a good idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I almost like it enough for an upvote, but the whale extinction thing rubbed me the wrong way - not that they attempted it, but that they succeeded. So no vote for now. YMMV.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ostensibly this frequency resonates with quarks or alters gluons so that they are no longer bound by or convey the strong force, without which matter falls apart at a fundamental level (i.e. disintegrates into lone quarks).

The resulting energy release has a similar effect on other quarks/gluons, sort of like tin pest. Which means this could easily destroy the Earth/solar system/universe as we know it, depending on the limits of the chain reaction.

At least that's how I interpret it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm intentionally keeping it unanswered how the Foundation found out about this set of frequencies. I like the notion that the threat is purely theoretical, but that the Foundation is too terrified to actually test it.

I'm also pleased that now, whenever you hear a fluourescent light or a speaker hum, in the back of your mind you'll remember that this is because the Foundation is desperately trying to save the world…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If this were added to the article, I'd be a lot happier with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This comment just changed my no-vote to an upvote. My one problem is gone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 to you good sir

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nah, if the fact that this thing will blow up the entire planet is stored with it everywhere, that's some security right there. There's very few people who would want to physically destroy the planet, and AWCY do not number among them.

Incidentally, there's no sensible way for this to chain react. Sound is extremely easily disrupted by any vibration, and any sort of destruction will cause massive vibrations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To hell with the GOC — this is something the Foundation might ally with MC&D to contain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The thing about aircraft is, they're surrounded by air.
Letting AWCY even know about the existence of SCP-1012 would have to be averted at all costs- up to and including H-bombing cities in which suspected AWCY members might be.

It occurs to me that SCP-1012 is something that the Foundation might ally with the GOC to contain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As for how they would figure it out, it more just feels like something they would find interesting to some degree. I could see them broadcasting it over a frequency to hit an aircraft. Hah.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How would they figure this out? And how would they do it without blowing everything up? And why would they want to destroy the world? Even if we assume they were behind 804, that still doesn't quite match with trying to destroy the whole world.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm…. You know, this might be a good opportunity to use Are We Cool Yet? in an article as a developer, if you're interested, Spike.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't like it either, but I like this enough to ignore it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ^This is the one and only thing keeping me from upvoting this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After discussing in chat, my theory is that some researchers decided to see how they could develop a sonic-based weapon. Upon discovering what *this* combination of frequencies would do, they promptly recommended doing anything possible to keep them from ever being made at the same time.
So, purely theoretical, but still being contained by necessity. I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Much improved, Spike. Good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I will sing the song that ends the earth.
